title: Bryanthus aleuticus
intro: Bryanthus aleuticus, also known as Aleutian cranberry or Aleutian heather, is a low-growing, evergreen shrub native to the Aleutian Islands in Alaska. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including urinary tract infections and inflammation, due to its potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties. Bioactive constituents of Bryanthus aleuticus include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Bryanthus aleuticus are teas and infusions, as well as extracts, which are often used to support urinary health and reduce inflammation.
taxonomy: Bryanthus aleuticus is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Ericaceae. It is a type of low-growing shrub native to the Aleutian Islands in Alaska. The exact classification of Bryanthus aleuticus can also be expressed as a member of the genus Bryanthus, which is a group of flowering plants within the Ericaceae family. Bryanthus aleuticus has a complex taxonomic classification, and it is often considered to be closely related to other plants in the Ericaceae family, such as Rhododendron and Vaccinium. Bryanthus aleuticus is further classified as a member of the Ericales order within the Angiospermophyta division.
uses: Bryanthus aleuticus, also known as Aleutian rose, is a plant native to the Aleutian Islands in Alaska. There is limited information available about its medicinal uses. parts: Bryanthus aleuticus is a species of flowering plant, but information on its medicinal properties and specific parts used for medicinal purposes is not readily available.
